vulnerability.title: Record Events that Modify User/Group Information - /etc/group
vulnerability.description: If the auditd daemon is configured to use the augenrules program to read audit rules during daemon startup (the default), add the following lines to a file with suffix .rules in the directory /etc/audit/rules.d: -w /etc/group -p wa -k audit_rules_usergroup_modification If the auditd daemon is configured to use the auditctl utility to read audit rules during daemon startup, add the following lines to /etc/audit/audit.rules: -w /etc/group -p wa -k audit_rules_usergroup_modification
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q audit && rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then # Perform the remediation for both possible tools: 'auditctl' and 'augenrules' # Create a list of audit *.rules files that should be inspected for presence and correctness # of a particular audit rule. The scheme is as follows: # # ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- # Tool used to load audit rules | Rule already defined | Audit rules file to inspect | # ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- # auditctl | Doesn't matter | /etc/audit/audit.rules | # ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- # augenrules | Yes | /etc/audit/rules.

triage_decision.reason: Adding an audit rule to monitor changes to /etc/group is a standard configuration change that does not affect authentication, networking, or filesystem operation. It follows STIG hardening patterns for audit rules and carries no risk of service lockout or system breakage.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan correctly adds the required audit rule for monitoring /etc/group, handling both augenrules and auditctl configurations. It ensures no duplicate entries and addresses the vulnerability. Minor enhancements, such as reloading the audit daemon after changes, would improve reliability.
concerns: No immediate audit daemon reload step is included; the new rule will take effect only after a reboot or when auditd reloads the rule files manually.
The script assumes the presence of sed and may not handle edge cases of existing comments containing similar patterns.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan adds the required audit rule to both augenrules and auditctl configurations and avoids duplicate entries in audit.rules. However, it lacks a restart/reload of auditd to activate the changes, and it overwrites any custom content in the rules file instead of merging, which could remove other important rules. It also doesn't explicitly set file permissions or ownership.
concerns: Audit daemon is not reloaded/ restarted after rule changes, delaying enforcement.
If /etc/audit/rules.d/00_usergroup_modification.rules already exists with additional rules, the script will overwrite the file, potentially removing those rules.
The created rule file may inherit default permissions/ownership that are not optimal (should be 0640 root:root).
suggested_improvements: Add a step to reload auditd (e.g., systemctl reload auditd or auditctl -R) after changes.
Modify the logic to append or update the rule without wiping existing content (use grep/ed or cat >>).
Explicitly set file permissions to 0640 and ownership to root:root after creation.
Consider handling the case where /etc/audit/audit.rules does not exist gracefully.
